What's up everybody? Okay so what we're going to do in this video is cover the why, who, when and what. Alright we're going to cover how and where because each of those is much longer but we're going to cover this first four right now. So why? Why measure business intelligence? Why measure metrics? Why have business intelligence? Why is it worth the time investment that it's going to take to gather this data and display it and do calculations on a daily and weekly basis? Because if I'm telling you to do something then there has to be an ROI for it right? So why do we do this? First off is identifying areas of improvements measurably. So people are like I want to grow my business, I want to grow my business. The best way to grow is by removing constraints. A lot of times it's counterintuitive, a lot of times you grow like I need to get better at sales marketing but usually that's not the thing that's holding back. To give you a fitness example if you're really good at deadlifting right or you're sorry, you're trying to build your deadlift and you're really strong off the floor but your grip keeps giving out right? It doesn't matter if you get stronger, like if your grip keeps giving out and then you start using straps to train and get your posterior chain stronger, that's still not going to increase your deadlift because your grip is the weakness. And so you could probably not work on the other stuff at all, barely maintain it and just work on your grip strength and all of a sudden your deadlift will shoot through the roof because it's the constraint, it's the weak link in your business that is holding it back. And so having business intelligence allows you to intelligently identify the area that is your weakest link, which allows you to break through bottlenecks. And one of the big benefits is having realistic goals and forecasting. So one of the things that we've received a lot of praise on is that we've scaled at an unbelievably fast rate with our company. And in order to do that we've had to forecast what revenue is going to look like, what expenses are going to look like, what staffing is going to look like and anticipate those needs so that we can be ahead of the curve. And the only way to anticipate that is analytics, is information, is data. And so if you know how to use this to your advantage you will out-compete your competition because you will make smarter decisions. In my opinion data is the most important business asset to the owner. It's the most valuable thing that we have because it is the thing that informs our decisions and that is our job is to make decisions. And if you have poor quality data you make poor decisions. So it would behoove us to get the right data. Poor data equals good decisions. And there's a reason that military like rely on intel, intelligence. There's a reason it's called intelligence is because you make intelligent decisions when you have data. So they have massive networks of spies and human intelligence and signal intelligence and drones and all sorts of things to collect data, to take pictures, to do everything they can so that one piece of information can change the course of a war. And so if you think about it within the context of business there may be one weak link that can change the entire context of your business future. And I mean that genuinely because sometimes when you identify these weak links I've seen it time and time again where businesses explode once they realize what the weak link is and you can't know it until you measure everything. And so I just said that but business is making mathematical decisions with the context of humans.com. So who is supposed to do this? In the beginning it's going to be you because you need to understand this more than anyone because it is your business. So in the beginning it is going to be you. You're going to be the one tracking it, you're going to be the one analyzing it, making decisions, etc. After that it's going to be your ops manager. So that's going to be your operations manager. If you don't have one that is going to be probably your next hire after you hire trainers. And what that person is going to do is they're going to track it and collect it. You should still be the one kind of reporting it to your team and interpreting the data for them so they understand what's good, what's bad, what's baseline so that they know what to improve on. So like this is really important. You need to be able to articulate and talk about these things with confidence and that's what we're going to cover in these modules. But first it's you and then it's your operations manager. They're the one who are going to track these things. They're the one who's going to collect the data and put it in one place where you can see it all at once. And after that once your business gets bigger the team then tracks their own data and reports it into a centralized database. The operations manager will report it to you and then you will report it to the whole team. So as the business becomes more layered there are multiple communication lines that happen to communicate and analyze data. Fun tip here. Then I'm going to be giving you an Excel sheet at the end of this that will have all the metrics that I recommend that you track. Just because I've done this. You don't want to accidentally delete it or have an employee accidentally delete it and all of a sudden all of your business intelligence, all of your historical data disappears. And so at the end of each week copy your data template which I'm going to provide you at the end and put it into a separate data sheet that only you control. And I would definitely recommend saving it in the cloud or as a Google Drive document so that it never gets lost. And do you do this. So different metrics should be reported on on different cadences. So it doesn't make sense to report profit on a daily basis because costs don't come in that evenly. So there's four kind of cadences that you can do this on and I'm going to break down each of these. So you have daily which is going to be kind of on your huddles that you have. There are going to be some stats that you should report every day. Specifically I'm going to give you the ones that are recommended to kind of like the default or the cannon. But the things that you start every day that you measure every day are going to be things that get the most attention. And so if you want to work on one particular aspect of your business you can cycle those stats in and out of your daily huddle. So you want to have the basics in there but if you want to talk a lot about retention every day then talk about your exits every day. If you really want to focus more on average ticket or like cash up front because that's what you want your team to focus on improving then you report on that every day. So each of these stats can be cycled in and out of the daily huddles to emphasize whatever aspect of the business is your weak link until it gets improved. Beyond the daily there are some stats that are better that I think suited slightly better for weekly because snapshots on a day to day basis sometimes will give very skewed impressions very good or very bad which doesn't really give you a whole picture. So weekly there are some times and that's going to be when you're meeting with your team so you want to make sure that you have this data beforehand so you can report to them and analyze it and give them clear interpretation of what it means. And then there's some data that's monthly. Like a lot of times like profit on a monthly basis is probably useful to do it at that point because there's a lot of bills that come once a month. And so to accurately you know it's like the first of the month is when you have all your bills and you're like well we're negative this month this week super negative in the next three weeks you're overwhelmingly positive. I don't know if that's a clear picture for the team all of a sudden one week per month they think they're sucking so I would probably do that stat for example on a monthly basis. But you're going to need to use your discernment when we go over the stats but think about the cadences of when it would make the most sense. I'll have recommendations but just to understand when reporting them and collecting makes the most sense. And then I say quarterly here but quarterly and yearly I think yearly planning is sometimes really difficult. I like quarterly because things change so quickly and your bottlenecks are going to be very different. And so that basically gives you 12 weeks to focus on something that you really want to improve. One component of the business that you want to massively improve and then set objectives around that. And a lot of times your quarterly goals will affect what your daily reporting stats are so that your team can focus on that new objective for the defined period of time. Note the best way to get your team to focus on these reported data. So I'm saying it again because it's important. And then what? So what buckets do these intelligence metrics fall in? So there's three primary buckets that these all fall in. The first is acquisition. So that's going to be all stats related to inflow of customers, new customers, new signups, etc. The second bucket is going to be fulfillment. So these are all the stats related to customer life cycles after the sale. So acquisition is lead gen, lead nurture sales, boom, acquisition. Fulfillment is everything kind of after that point is what percentage of people are converting. What's the average EFT? What's the average price? What's the lifetime value? What's our churn? All of those things would be stats that would fall under fulfillment or trigger and reflect how well we're doing on keeping our promises. And the third is sort of financial. What I mean by that is larger business context, what are we doing money wise? These are the three major buckets that all the metrics kind of fall into. So I split them up this way so that you can kind of think about it from that angle. I think it was the easiest way to break it up. So all stats related to financial health of a business as a whole. And then a special note here is that this is per business function slash revenue stream. So if you have multiple revenue streams, then you're going to want to have metrics around acquisition, fulfillment and financial for each of those streams. And I'm talking categories of service. Not like, I mean, you can drill down. So I don't want to, I don't want to, like those of you who are data junkies like I am, by all means, if you want to drill down into like each type of service that you offer and you want to cover all of these different things, that is fine. The more data you have, typically the better, as long as it's valuable to informed decision making. Okay. That is the point of collecting data. If you just collect data to collect data, that's stupid and it's a waste of time. That is not intelligence. The data that you should have, each piece of data should be important to inform a decision that is going to be relevant to making more money and providing more value to the customers and identifying weak spots. Okay. That is why we do this. That is why the certain stats that I've selected are selected. There's a bazillion different statistics you can grab. The ones that I have are the ones that I would use and that I use still most frequently in our business. Okay. So, most of the people we're watching this have two primary revenue streams. One will be service, the other will be retail and physical products. And so, we break down both of those things into acquisition, fulfillment, and financial. All right. Sweet. So, let's get on to the next one.
